original_abstract: Modern society has long acknowledged the distinct status of children within legal and judicial frameworks. As the law inevitably reflects evolving social values, recent systemic shifts have been driven by an increased awareness of the need to adapt normative and institutional structures to accommodate the specific vulnerabilities of the child. Such protections are paramount within the repressive arms of the state, specifically criminal and misdemeanor law. Adopting the contemporary terminology of juvenile criminal law, this paper examines the trajectory of the "child in conflict with the law." We provide a comprehensive overview of the normative foundations and future perspectives regarding children’s legal standing, analyzing both supranational standards and their implementation within the Croatian national context. Furthermore, the discussion extends beyond the child as an offender to address the critical position of the child as a victim of crime.
humanized_abstract: Children as a category have been recognized as a special group in modern society in both criminal law and criminology. The ongoing transformation of social relations and the resulting adjustment of the normative and institutional framework to children’s specific needs are among the factors that have caused children to be dealt with differently in criminal law. As part of the repressive apparatus of the state – criminal and misdemeanor law – children are dealt with according to the principles of the modern juvenile criminal law, referred to today as the child in conflict with the law. This paper gives an overview of the current standards for the legal status of children and provides insights into their implementation at the national level. Besides dealing with the child as an offender, we shall also focus on the position of the child as a crime victim.

This paper gives an overview of the current standards for the legal status of children and provides insights into their implementation at the national level. Besides dealing with the child as an offender, we shall also focus on the position of the child as a crime victim.
